narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, you've put significant effort into your path toward law school, including your undergraduate studies and LSAT preparation. The admissions model we use evaluates applicants based on key indicators from past students' data, predicting the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, it estimated a 58% chance of failing the bar exam, which crossed the threshold that led to your application's rejection—essentially, the school aims to admit those with stronger predicted odds of bar success to maintain high pass rates. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is notably lower than the typical 5th or 6th decile for admitted students, signaling to the model a potential struggle in advanced coursework that often foreshadows bar challenges. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, just below the average of around 3.21, actually worked in your favor by suggesting decent foundational academic strength compared to others. Your choice to pursue part-time status, less common than the full-time path taken by over 90% of students, nudged the prediction slightly toward failure, as the model associates it with divided focus that impacts bar outcomes. Your LSAT score of 27, well below the usual 36-37 range, provided some counterbalance by pushing toward a pass prediction, though not enough to offset the top concerns. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile of 3rd had a minor influence toward failure, and your middle family income bracket offered a small positive tilt. Overall, your profile stands out as having weaker late-stage high school performance and an unconventional part-time plan compared to the stronger, full-time trajectories of typical successful applicants. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-class background—many in similar situations have succeeded with targeted improvements. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic deciles through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and exploring full-time options if feasible. We encourage you to reapply after addressing these areas, as admissions models like this one reward clear progress in the key predictors of bar success. Keep pushing; your determination can change the story.
